Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit unconventional. I'd dedicate about 24 hours a week to studying, but I'd often do it in intense bursts rather than spreading it evenly. I preferred reading and writing things out, so I'd download materials and work through them on my own time. I found that I could be productive even when I wasn't physically logged into the system.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was coming up. When deadlines approached, I'd ramp up significantly, but during quieter weeks I might not touch the LMS at all. My attendance wasn't great at 44%, but that doesn't mean I wasn't learning—I just found other ways to keep up with the content that worked better for my anxiety levels.
Q3: Usually it was either an assignment deadline looming or needing to check if new materials had been posted. Sometimes I'd get a notification about something and that would prompt me to log in. I wasn't someone who browsed the LMS casually—I went in with a specific purpose and got out once I had what I needed.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. I'd remember to check it when I needed something specific, but it wasn't like checking my phone or email. The gamified tasks they introduced actually helped a bit—they made logging in feel less like a chore and more like something I could accomplish.
Q5: The reading materials and written guides were most useful to me since that's how I learn best. I'd download PDFs and lecture notes and really dig into them. The video content was helpful too—I watched quite a bit of it—but I often found myself taking written notes from the videos rather than just passively watching.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums. I only posted twice the whole course, which I know isn't great. It's partly my anxiety—putting my thoughts out there publicly feels uncomfortable. I also didn't see as much value in them compared to just working through the material myself or occasionally talking to peers directly.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it connected to what I'd be assessed on or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. The gamified elements actually helped here—earning points or completing challenges made even routine tasks feel more purposeful. I need to feel like I'm making progress.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford mentally. With my anxiety, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. Required activities got priority because the consequences of skipping them were clear. Optional stuff I'd only do if I genuinely found it interesting or if I had extra mental bandwidth that week.
Q9: Absolutely, this was a huge part of how I studied. I'd download everything I could and work offline with my notes, textbooks, and printed materials. I'd rewrite key concepts, create summaries, and test myself using methods that wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. A lot of my 24 study hours happened completely offline.
Q10: There were entire study sessions where I'd be working from printed notes or downloaded PDFs without any internet connection. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person—we'd quiz each other and discuss concepts. None of that peer interaction showed up in my LMS stats, even though I had about 14 meaningful interactions with peers overall.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in once, download everything available, and then not return until new content was posted. My video watch time might look high, but honestly, I often watched things once and then relied on my written notes afterward. The LMS would have no idea I was still actively engaging with that material days later.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my anxiety spiked and engaging felt impossible. Even though I'd describe my stress as generally low, anxiety is a different beast. When multiple assignments piled up or when I felt uncertain about expectations, I'd sometimes freeze up and avoid the LMS entirely for a few days.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. I'm involved in extracurriculars, and sometimes those commitments meant I couldn't log in as regularly as I should have. Fatigue was a factor too—after an intense study session offline, the last thing I wanted was more screen time on the LMS.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open while my mind was elsewhere, just going through the motions. Those sessions might register as activity, but they weren't productive. I learned to recognize when I was doing this and would step away instead.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts because they triggered my anxiety. I knew participating was important, but the thought of putting my ideas out there for everyone to see was intimidating. I'd tell myself I'd do it later, and then later never came. Assignments I could do privately felt much more manageable.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary driver for LMS activity. I'd check in more frequently as due dates approached, making sure I had everything I needed. My assignment completion rate was over 90%, so I always got things done—I just tended to do the actual work in concentrated bursts close to the deadline rather than spread out.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me to engage more when it was constructive. Getting a good score or positive comments made me feel like my approach was working, even if it looked unconventional from the outside. Negative feedback would sometimes increase my anxiety, but my resilience helped me bounce back and use it to improve.
Q18: The course structure influenced me quite a bit. When modules were clearly organized with downloadable materials, I thrived. When things required constant LMS presence or real-time participation, I struggled more. The gamified tasks they introduced actually helped give me structure and small wins to work toward.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement much easier for me. What made it harder was when participation required being visible or social, like discussion forums. The gamified elements were a nice middle ground—they gave me goals to achieve without the social pressure of public participation.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or uncertain about what to submit. They might be checking materials repeatedly, trying to understand expectations, but feeling too unsure to actually turn something in. Or they could be perfectionists who keep reviewing but never feel ready. Activity doesn't equal confidence.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience pretty well. My LMS engagement level was technically low, but I earned a Merit grade. I was doing tons of work offline—reading, writing, processing information in ways that suited my learning style. The system just couldn't see most of my actual studying.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and what you submitted. But they miss the depth of engagement—whether you actually understood what you read, how much you studied offline, or the quality of your thinking. They also can't capture anxiety or the mental effort behind each action.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material and trying to make it stick. It's not about how often I log in—it's about whether I'm genuinely wrestling with concepts, connecting ideas, and working toward understanding. For me, engagement often happens in my head and in my notes, not on a screen.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually cared about doing well despite my anxiety. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials, the notes I wrote by hand, or the conversations with classmates that happened outside the system. It also wouldn't show that my low activity was a coping strategy, not disengagement.
